sentences.text: Well, it's not a-- it's not a place.
What you're doing is that you're in a big forest.
And you're moving to different sections of the forest.
So it's not like saying, one day we were in here, in this town.
And one day-- there was no town to speak of.
It was just a huge forest.
And you move from place to place.
So I have no names to give you.
I don't even know the name of the forest.
But you're on the move because if you stood still, you were caught.
And so when I say we were constantly on the move, we were running around like-- so as not to be in one place for very long.
There was no-- you did what everybody else did.
There was no-- you know, there was an adult next to if there was a younger person.
I was the only child with them at the time.
It was a small group of-- I think there were maybe two or three women with them.
That's all.
And of course, the women would kind of keep an eye on me or hand me the food.
But it wasn't a question of being taken care of.
It's-- you learn to, to walk when everybody else walked, to sit when everybody else sits, to be very quiet when they tell you to be very quiet.
And that included all the grownups and, and me.
So it's, it's, it's very hard to describe.
It really is because it sounds almost like a story, I mean, a little kid running around with grown-ups in a battle-type of situation, but that's what it was.
And when they felt it was safe for me to return to the farmhouse, they returned me to the farmhouse.
Did you want to return?
Yes and no.
For some strange reason I felt closer to, to my uncles who were not in the group because I was with the partisans.
But I knew that I couldn't stay with them.
This was a, a fact.
So I went back to the farmhouse.
No, not in that group.
No.
I don't.
No.
They were not.
They were-- I don't even know if there was Jew among them, this particular group.
There were Jews in the-- in the partisans, my uncles, Grisha Rozenblat who is-- was very decorated and very courageous.
There were others that I don't recall all their names.
But I met some of them when I was with my uncle after he took me from the farmhouse.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The sad part of it is that even in the forests, they, they didn't welcome the Jews to be part of the resistance.
I, I know that some of them were accepted because they could translate because very few of the Russians or the Ukrainians who were communists were part of the partisans.
Because the majority of the Ukrainians sided with the Germans.
They felt that they would be liberated from communism.
So the few Ukrainians and mostly the Russians who were the partisans, they didn't speak German.
So for many reasons, they accepted some of the Jews, but not open-heartedly, not as it's our-- you know, it's your war, too.
So and, and most of-- most of the young men died, were taken away.
And so there were very few.
No.
I don't.
I had asked my uncle about it when I was in Israel.
And he couldn't remember their name either.
They were just a family that they had been in the ghetto.
And I guess God works in strange ways that we should cross paths in such a strange way and that they should run into him.
They-- we weren't friends.
He didn't remain friends.
I don't know whether they stayed in Russia or whether they emigrated anywhere.
We had no idea.
It was just a passing of people, which happened a lot in those days.
You saw somebody that you used to know.
And you'd say, did you see this one?
Did you see that one?
And it was-- it wasn't that you became friends afterwards.
Some did.
Some didn't.
What do you recall about the actual liberation?
Oh, I remember that for several days we heard tremendous, you know, thunder.
And the-- we, we felt that the Russians were coming.
And very shortly after that, the Russians did take that part of the, the-- where I was on the farm.
And they stayed for a while.
They were in the-- in the-- in the yard of the farmhouse.
They relaxed.
They ate.
They-- maybe a week or two.
And then, they marched forward.
And after that, they-- things came back to more or less the way they were before without the German patrols.
The light went on.
We got up in the morning.
We fed the-- and went until the day my uncle came.
I was in the fields with the cows.
And I saw three men and a woman walking towards the farmhouse.
And I looked.
And in my heart, I saw it was my uncle.
But my head would not accept it.
I saw them go to the farmhouse.
And a little while later, Anna came and got me.
And she said, look, she said, your uncle came.
And I didn't want to go near him.
I-- he said, don't you remember me?
And I didn't answer him.
And he couldn't-- he came over.
And he put his arms around me.
And I, I knew it was him.
And I think he started to cry.
I-- he stayed the night.
And I wouldn't go with him.
I don't know why.
I guess-- I don't know why.
But he left.
And then, he came back a week or so later and with a new dress for me.
And the Anna and he and Grisha and I went on the train.
And she left me there with him.
I-- it took me about a week, I think.
Then I accepted things as they were.
And I-- in my heart, I'd always known he was my uncle.
So my uncle Sana and Grisha and [?
Fanni ?] and I, we lived in a house in Kiev for several months.
And he enrolled me in school.
And he talked to me, held me a lot, which was the first time in a long time anyone had held me.
He explained to me that the part of, of the world where we came from was in Russian hands again.
And he wanted to go and see if any of our family survived.
We were aware that the immediate family that had come with us into that part of the world was gone.
He knew my grandfather had been killed.
He knew it.
He knew my Uncle Chaim was dead.
He knew that my Aunt Chava and my parents were together, and they had been killed.
But he said, maybe there was somebody left in Poland.
We were-- we had a large family.
There were so many cousins and aunts and uncles.
And he said, maybe somebody's left.
And he told me to-- Grisha and [?
Fanni ?] would take care of me.
And he promised me he would come back.
He was only going to see.
So he left me with them, and he went.
And he went to the-- and he went to Kurow, and there was nothing there.
So there is no Kurow anymore.

He was waiting in the railroad station for the train to go back-- to go back to Kiev when a man from the town where we come from saw him and ran up to him and hugged him and said, have you seen your sister?
Have you seen your sister?
And my uncle said, what sister?
So he told him, your sister and her husband, Harry, and their two little girls are alive.
I'll take you to them.
So he found what was then my aunt and uncle and their two little girls.
And he stayed with them for a while, talking I guess and telling their stories to each other.
And he came back for me.
And he left me with them to go to Israel because he was, as I said before, running from the Russians at that time because they wouldn't give him permission to move to Poland.
And he went with other young men through Cyprus on Aliyah Bet into Israel.
My, my mother, who was then my aunt, took me into her family and raised me as one of their own.
We lived in Lublin.
There were three families living in an apartment.
It was a long apartment, and we had the middle room.
There was room for two beds and a table.
I remember there were people there for dinner.
Us children sat underneath because there was no other place to sit.
The war was still going on.
Lublin was liberated.
But the, the front was in Warsaw.
This was in the fall of 1944, September, October.
Give us the names of your aunt and uncle and their two daughters.
Harry, Helen Kotlar, their daughters, Gloria and Betty.
They're both married.
Well, my sister Gloria, she's a widow now.
Her name is Gloria Kagasoff.
And my sister Betty and long life to her husband, Lou Wiener.
They have children.
They have grandchildren.
Where did you go after Lublin?
We moved to Lodz.
From Lublin, we moved to Lodz.
And we lived in Lodz in, in part of '45 and part of '46.
We went to school there.
And we ran into anti-Semitism in school because they taught Catholicism.
And we were asked to be excused from the hour where they talked catechisms and things.
So they realized we were Jewish.
And things were not easy after that in school.
And my family realized that there was no place to stay in Poland.
And we wanted to get out.
My, my aunt and uncle-- whom I call mom and dad, so when I say mom and dad, you'll know.
They-- my, my dad had family here in America.
And my, my mother's two-- we knew we had one, one brother in Israel, the one that left in 1939.
So we decided that wherever-- whether it Israel or America, we would not stay in Europe, that it was no place for Jews anymore.
So we, we lived in Lodz.
And in 1946, we pretended we were going on vacation and just packed suitcases as if you were going on vacation.
And we took to Czechoslovakia.
From Czechoslovakia, we went into Germany.
And in Germany, we went to a DP camp near Munich called [NON-ENGLISH].
And we stayed there for a while.
But then my father, my dad, decided that it might be easier for us if we went out of the DP camp to have papers, try to get papers through HIAS and his brothers in America to come to America.
So we moved to Stuttgart.
And we lived in Stuttgart in part of 1946 into 1947 when we received our papers to go to the United States.
We came to the United States April 1, 1947.
Oh, yes.
We had a harrowing journey.
We boarded ship in-- first of all, they isolated us for a period of time, maybe as long as two months because of all the shots and things.
They wanted to be sure that no one was ill.
And we boarded a, a ship on March 10, I think it was or somewhere around there called the SS Ernie Pyle.
It was a troop ship.
But a short time out into the ocean, they had some trouble.
So they had to turn back.
And we went-- we went back to-- we were-- they stayed outside the English waters in England.
So we had to wait for another ship to take that ship's refugees to the United States and come back for us.
So we, we crossed over a plank of water from one ship to the other.
The ship that brought us to the United States was the SS Marilyn Marie.
And we arrived in, in New York April 1, 1947.
And my father had an uncle here from the early years before World War II-- before WWI, I think.
And he had been ill, so he wasn't at the dock to meet us.
But his brother Rudy and his and Aunt Ray were there at the dock to meet us.
And there were-- there were 900 and some passengers on the boat.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That's when I first had spaghetti or an orange, saw American sailors.
There's-- I have a neighbor who came on the same ship with me.
We found out many years later.
It's been fine.
Oh, I have a-- I have a husband.
I have two children.
We have a home.
It's fine.
I met my husband through a friend who knew him several years through-- his best friend.
Anita introduced Hal and I.
And we dated probably about a year or so, got engaged, got married.
We were married in January '56, January 21, 1956.
We were married at the Hollywood Knickerbocker in Hollywood.
My husband was born in Chicago in 1933, April 22, 1933.
I have a daughter, Susan.
She was born April 24, 1959.
And I have a son, Jeffrey.
He was born January 30, 1962.
And he lives in Chicago.
So we talk on the phone.
But we don't get to see each other too much.
Well, I was a private secretary in my youth.
I've-- I worked part-time several times when the children were in school, mainly in offices.
I love to read.
I'm an avid reader.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Let me think, what else do I like to do?
I love music.
I, I tried to knit.
And I couldn't.
And that's about it, I think.
What do you do these days?
My husband and I opened a business in 1979.
And I work in our store with him.
I help people.
I answer the phone.
I do a little bookkeeping.
It's what I call a gofer.
Actually, I didn't want her to leave.
I guess she was my anchor for those couple years.
But she left during the night when I was asleep.
So there was no goodbye, goodbye.
During the couple of days that she stayed, she said that my uncle had come for me, that I was going to live with him now.
And she couldn't leave all the animals alone.
And, you know, and I would go to school and things like that, where there was no school on the farm.
But she left when I was asleep, so I don't know whether I would have caused a scene or not.
Yes, I do.

I'm sorry that the circumstances made it so that I cannot remember her last name if it was ever told to me, that I couldn't write to her to say thank you for her care of me, to maybe help her in some way, you know, not exactly the way she helped me but somehow do something.
But I don't know how.
And I've wished her well all my life.

I would like to say thank you very much for taking me in, for caring for me, for doing the few little things that she had the time to do with me-- the walks, the showing me how to feed the chickens, the, the bathing, the taking care, the things that a child needs from a grownup.
And I appreciate all that.
I don't know if she came right out and told me.
I don't remember.
I know that she said that my mother and father had told me that they would be back in a while, but circumstances were such that they couldn't come back.
And I think that from what she said, I knew because we knew.
And when they came and told us about my Uncle Chaim, I think that that was the loneliest day of my life because at that point, I didn't know if anyone knew where I was.
I remember, I went out and sat on a rock.
And [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I think in a way, I was angry that I was left behind.
And because we were told that there were two Jews in the group that died.
And I assumed that his brother was with him.
And that I was angry with him for having his brother with him.
And why didn't he come and get me?
And you know, things, thoughts like that went through your head or my head.
And I really felt all alone.
I-- I think that day I cried, whereas I didn't cry the time before.
He, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was special to me.
And I think I felt that as long as he was alive, everybody else was OK.
And that seemed to end it.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So it wasn't-- it was a bad day.
Yes, I do.
I, I think that at the beginning, people didn't want to hear.
And a lot of us were silent because we didn't want to make people feel bad.
I think it was the greatest mistake we ever made, the survivors.
There were so few of us who spoke out.
The, the-- And I think it was a terrible mistake because we have a generation who knows so little about it.
Some won't accept it even today.
And I feel that it's very important for the younger people to know what happened.
There were whole families wiped out.
I myself made a list in Israel for Yad Vashem of 137 people on one side of the family.
[SOBBING] My uncles had children.
There were young people who never got a chance to marry.
There were-- my little schoolmates, whom I don't know any of them are alive.
People should know.
People should be aware that humanity is capable of things that are horrible if the rest of the world pretends it isn't happening.
